Farn Polrrrcel PRACrrcEs CoulrrssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA 95914-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 25,2007
Joan Spencer
Planning Commissioner
City of Gilroy
7195 Yorktown Dr.
Gilroy, CA95020
RE:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-07-005
Dear Ms. Spencer:
This letter is in_ response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act,).' ii",rr*"g l, tiir'i"tt., should
be construed to evaluat e any conduct that may have already taken pla-ce, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective ""t;;;: 
preasJnot" trrut
our advice issued in response to your requestis timitei to obligation. u.iring;nder the
Act' In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair political practices
Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Does the Act prohibit you, as an appointed Planning commissioner for the City of
Gilroy, from participating in decisionr ."gr.ding a development project if you have a
business relationship with and you are daiing the applicani,s spokesperson?
CONCLUSION
No' The Act's conflicts-of-interest provisions regulate only public officials who
have a financial interest in a govenmental decision. Yoi have not offered any facts that
indicate you have a financial interest in the governmental decision. Further, glrts ora
' Go'emm""t Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 The commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 1g32g(bxsxA),
copy enclosed.)
@E


File No. 4-07-005
page No. 2
personal nature received in a"bota fide dating relationship,, are notsubject to the
reporting or gift limitation provisions of the Act. Accordingly, under thl facts presented,
the Act does not prevent you from participating in the decision.
FACTS
You are an appointed member of the City of Gilroy Planning Commission (the
"Planning Commission"). A development project will come before you on Februaiy 1,
2007. Chris Cot6, the spokesperson for the applicant, Gilroy Aviation, is a personal
friend of yours, as well as a dating partner.
Chris Cotd created Gilroy Aviation, but no longer owns the company; it is owned
in trust by two other individuals. You have stated that you are dating Nrfi. iote. you and
Mr. Cot6 are not married, nor do you have any joint possessionr, und you do not reside
together.
Prior to your appointment to the Planning Commission, you built a website for
Mr. Cot6 for which you were not paid any commission, hourly wage,or otherwise
compensated. You continue to pay the costs to maintain the website. Mr. Cot6
reimburses you for those out-of-pocket expenses. You want to know if the money that he
pays you as reimbursement for funds you pay to maintain the website creates a conflict of
interest and whether you will be able to vote on this project or if you must disqualifu
yourself.
Additionally, you have stated that in the past, the two of you have traveled on
vacation together, where you each paid for different parts of the trip. you wish to know
if any payments associated with this travel would create a conflict of interest for you.
In telephone calls we had with you on January 9 and 19,2007, you stated that you
do not receive any income for the work that you do for Mr. Cot6 and thut yo., are not
employed in any capacity by his business. Regarding the traveling you have done
together, you stated that you "went dutch," with him payrng for parts of the vacation, and
you paying for other parts. For example, you gave Mr. Cot6 some frequent flyer miles to
pay for his ticket and you paid for the rental car during the vacation and he paid other
expenses' You and Mr. Cot6 have been dating for at least three years, and have known
each other for about four years. During the course of your relatibnship, you have
exchanged gifts for birthdays and holidays.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that pubiic officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused Uy ttreir own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a..financial
interest" in a govemmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably


File No. 4-07-005
Page No. 3
foreseeable that the goveflrmental decision will have amateialfinancial effect on one or
more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;regulation 1g700(a).)
Absent an "economic interest" as defined in the Act, an official will not have a
prohibited or disqualifying conflict of interest. The commission has adopted an eight-
step standard analysis for deciding whether an individuuihu. u ai.qrrii$ng conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision. (Reguratio, iazooluxl)-(g).)
Steps one and two: Are you a government official making, participating in making,
or influencing a governmental decision?
As an appointed member of the Planning commission, you are a public official
under the Act. (Section 92049)3 consequeatly, you may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use your official position-to-influence any decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any oryo.r, economic interests.
(Regulations 18702;1-18702.4.) As a member of the Fluroirrg commission, you will be
called upon to consider a development project that "will inclute a Tentative Map,
Planned unit Development, Zoie chanee, and Architecture and site.,, Therefore, you
will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official position to
influence a govefilmental decision.
step three: Do you have a potentially disqualifying economic interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision ;;ffi#;-"dr,
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on th" frb11c generally, on the official, on
a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
1' An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Sectiin g7r03(a),reguration
18703 '1(a)') 
^An 
interest in any business entity in whi"t a public official is
a director' officer, partner, trustee, employee, tr hords any position of
management. (Section g7 103 (d), regulation 1 g703. 1 (b).i
2. An interest in rear property in which a public officiar has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Sectitn azr oilu;, reguration
18703.2.)
3 Section 87105 provides that when a public official who holds an office specified in section
87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed. at a pruri. **i*g, then he or she must: (1)
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identrf, ";bd" 
of economic interest involved in
the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 1s702.5(b),on the record
of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the .oorn ro, the duration of the discussion
and/or vote on the item- ror closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the
public regarding personal interests, special rules found ln."grtutiorr'1-voz.s1"1 and 1g702.5(d) apply.


File No. 4-07-005
Page No. 4
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the public
official that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 871 03(c), regulation 1 8703.3.)
4. Any source of gifts to the public officiar if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (section g7103(e),
regulation 18703.4.)
5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her
immediate family. This is also known as the "personal financial effects,,
rule. (Section 87103, regulation 18703.5.)
You have not presented any facts to indicate that you have any economic interest
in the applicant, Gilroy Aviation. Your first question concerns whether or not the
payments you have received from Mr. cot6 would, under the Act, impact your
participation the govemment decision you have identified, and whether any partof the
expenses incurred on the trip you took together constitutes a gift.
You have three types of economic interests that could potentially create a
disqualifying conflict of interest: income, business position, urd giftr. ih" a.t defines
"income" as a payment received, including any sarary, wage, o, udr*"". (section
82030(a)') A"gift" under the Act is any payment that confers a personal benefit on the
recipient, to the extent that consideration blequal or greater value is not received.
(Section 82028(a).)
Income
You state that Mr. Cot6 reimburses you for costs you incur to maintain a website
you built for him, but he did not pay you to build the website. Additionally, while you
continue to update the website for him, he does not pay you to do so. The definition of
"income" under the Act includes "reimbursement for expenses." (Section 82030(a).)
"Income," to be disqualifying, must aggregate to $500 or rnore during the twelve-month
period preceding the decision. (Regulation 18703.3(a)(1).) The reimbursements you
receive from Mr. Cot6 do not reach this threshold, however, because the total you receive
amounts to only $180 per twelve month period.
Business Position
You have a potential economic interest in Mr. Cot6's business if you are an
employee of that business. (Section 87103(d), regulation 18703(b).) Maintaining
Mr. cot6's website for no charge in itself do"r rroi constitute emplol.ment. Further, you
stated that you are not an employee of Mr. cot6's "development project.,,


File No. 4-07-005
Page No. 5
Gift
The payments you receive from Mr. Cot6 to maintain his website are not gifts to
you because a"gift" is a "payrnents that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." (Section 1ZOZA67.1
You put the charges to maintain Mr. Cot6's website, and in consideration, he refunds'you
that money. Because you provide consideration for the payment, it is not a gift.
Additionall_v,_you stated that you and Mr. Cot6 have traveled together, with each
of you paylng for different expenses associated with that travel. The Commission
recognizes that people in a "bona fide dating relationship" might exchange gifts of a
personal nature that do not fall within an enumerated exception, b.rt the Jisq"ualification
of which would not further the purpose of the Act- (Albuquerque Advice Litter,No. A-
00-1201' DeRosa Advice Letter, No. I-98-284.)
As defined by the Act, the term "gift" excludes gifts from an official,s immediate
family and certain specified relatives. 
_ (Section 82028(b)(3); regulatio n 1g9a2@)(3).)
The definition, however, does not exclude gifts from persons otLer than those speci frcally
identified. "Nevertheless, the Commission has advis "a 
thutpersonal gifts received within
an established bona fide dating relationship are not gifts under the Act. This type of
relationship is similar to a familial or spousal relationship, in which people frequently
exchange personal gifts and disclosure or disqualification would not further the purposes
of the Act." (Albuquerque, supra; DeRosa, supra.)
You and Mr. Cot6 have been dating for at least three years, and have been friends
for about four years. You exchange gifts on birthdays and other holidays, travel together,
and though yo.u do not reside together, you spend significant periods of time at each
other's home.a Based on these facts, it upp"u., thatihe gifts you have exchanged with
Mr. Cot6 ate of a personal nature, unrelated to your official business, and fall iltt i, th"
exception.r"$u111"g personal gifts exchanged between people in a bona fide dating
relationship.' (Alsop Advice Letter,No. A-03-032; Heiriig Advice Letter,No. 4_96_
162.)
Accordingly, under the facts you provided and based on the specific transactions
you listed (travel and reimbursements for the website), you do not have an economic
interest related to the governmental decision. For this.Luror, we need not analyze your
situation under the remaining steps four through eight. Under the limited facts provided,
nothing in the Act would disqualif, you from participating in the decisions regarding the
Gilroy Aviation development project.
a Regulation rc9a2@)(8) excepts gifts that are exchanged between a public official and another
person on holidays, birthdays, or similar occasions provided that the gifts exchanged are not substantially
disproportionate in value.
5 In such a relationship, truly personal gifts such as gifts of entertainment and meals enjoyed
together, certain personal property, or expenses involved in recreational travel together do not create a
conflict of interest and are not disqualifuing. (Herring, supra.) This exception does not apply, however, to
any gifts that are made in connection with efforts to influence governmental decisions. (Sie) Javor Advice
Letter, No. 78-088, copy enclosed.)


File No. A-07-005
Page No. 6
We are unable to offer you advice on bodies of law that, while may be relevant to
your situation, lie outside the Act and, therefore, beyond our jurisdiction, We refer you
instead to the Office of the Attorney General or to your City Attorney's office, which
may potentially provide you with informationregarding any incompatible activities that
may be pertinent to your decision.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
$rd{, ,t/t. (rn -.^
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
